summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's compliance with Japan's Mobile Software Competition Act (MSCA), which requires alternative app marketplaces, alternative payment options, browser/search engine choice screens, and other changes to iOS in Japan. He draws a sharp contrast between Japan's regulatory approach and the EU's Digital Markets Act (DMA), arguing that the MSCA is more focused, clearer, and more respectful of both Apple's intellectual property and user experience. Apple's tone toward Japan is notably cordial and cooperative, whereas its relationship with the European Commission is adversarial. Gruber notes that Japan gets alternative distribution and payment options without the trade-off of delayed or withheld features that EU users face. He concludes that practical results of regulation matter more than intentions, and Japan's approach delivers a better outcome for everyone involved.
key_points: Japan's MSCA requires alternative app marketplaces, payment options, and choice screens, but Apple views it as better balanced than the EU's DMA regarding privacy, security, and intellectual property
Apple's relationship with Japanese regulators is cooperative and respectful, in stark contrast to its adversarial stance toward the European Commission
Unlike in the EU, Apple has not delayed or withheld any features in Japan and does not expect to, because the MSCA targets specific competition issues without overreaching
Gruber argues that web link-outs from apps should be permitted without requiring Apple's IAP alongside them, but considers Japan's overall balance reasonable
Choice screens for browsers and search engines are ineffective theater that hasn't changed market share anywhere, but are a minor irritation at worst
money_quotes: Complying with the DMA is adversarial and obtuse.
There is a mutual respect here between Apple and Japan that is completely absent between Apple and the European Commission.
It arguably gives Japanese users a better, more robust iOS experience than what Apple offers to the rest of the world.
The DMA, in contrast, has given EU users a worse iOS experience.
It's the practical results of legislation and regulation that matter, not the intentions.

You can search, but you won't find quotes from Schiller, nor any other Apple representatives, speaking of their 'great respect for' and appreciation of the work they've done together regarding the European Commission and the DMA.
They're like a mandatory 'you should wear a helmet' warning label on a skateboard. You peel it off and throw it away, and the percentage of skateboarders who wear a helmet remains unchanged.
key_insight: Japan's MSCA demonstrates that tech regulation can open competition without degrading user experience, because it is focused, clear, and built on mutual respect — qualities entirely absent from the EU's DMA.
